nativeRange.habitat: Most often inhabits turbid, slow-moving waters with soft muddy substrates — inland lakes, swamps, marshes, and quiet pools of lowland rivers. Generally found over fine bottoms with detritus and filamentous algae rather than rocky reaches.

decorPreferences: soft sand substrate (they sift detritus and dig spawning pits)
smooth flat stones or slate as spawning sites
rooted and broad-leaved plants are tolerated — one of the few plant-safe Central American cichlids
subdued lighting and gentle to moderate flow

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy omnivore that leans toward detritus, filamentous algae, and small invertebrates in the wild. In the aquarium it tends to accept a wide range of foods, including quality cichlid pellets, flake, frozen bloodworms, frozen brine shrimp, and vegetable-based foods such as spirulina or blanched greens. A vegetable-rich diet tends to bring out the best colour.

goodWith: Peaceful community fish appropriate for warmer water (larger tetras, danios, rainbowfish, rasboras in a large enough tank)
Small to medium South American cichlids of similar temperament (e.g. keyhole cichlid)
Robust catfish such as Corydoras (in groups), bristlenose plecos, doradids
avoidWith: Aggressive Central American cichlids (convicts, Jack Dempseys, large amphilophus) that will dominate or kill them
Very small or slow long-finned fish during the breeding period when even peaceful pairs become territorial
Dwarf shrimp and small snails (likely eaten or harassed)
Other male rainbow cichlids in tanks too small to allow territories

imageCandidates.notes: Klaus Rudloff, 2100x1405. Sharp, vibrant golden-yellow adult showing the diagnostic broken black midlateral band; whole fish in frame against dark background. Small corner credit text only.
Uncredited (PD), 1706x1280. Correctly-ID'd Rainbow Cichlid with olive-gold body, broken black lateral band and caudal spot; slightly soft/motion-blurred so kept as non-recommended extra.

summary: The Rainbow Cichlid is a small, peaceful Central American cichlid notable for being one of the few species from the region that generally leaves aquarium plants undisturbed. It forms monogamous pairs and breeds readily on flat stones.
careNotes: The Rainbow Cichlid (Herotilapia multispinosa) is unusually peaceful for a Central American cichlid and is commonly recommended for planted community tanks — a distinction few cichlids from the region earn. Wild populations occur in slow, often turbid lowland lakes, swamps, and marshes from Honduras to Costa Rica, where the fish graze flocculent detritus, filamentous algae, diatoms, and small invertebrates. In the aquarium they tend to be calm outside of breeding periods and generally ignore well-established rooted plants; anubias, Java fern, swords, Vallisneria, and broad-leaved cryptocorynes are routinely kept alongside them without issue. Water parameters tend to be flexible: roughly 22–28 °C, pH 7.0–8.0, and moderately hard water (gH around 9–20) cover the typical care range, while wild populations have been reported to tolerate considerably warmer conditions — up to 33–36 °C in seasonal lowland pools. Adults are generally reported around 8–13 cm, with occasional larger males reaching 15–17 cm. A tank at least 90 cm long and around 150 L is a reasonable minimum for a pair, with additional length advised when keeping them alongside tankmates so the pair can hold a territory without harassing the rest of the community. Sexes are easy to distinguish at maturity: males are slightly larger with more pointed dorsal and anal fins, while ripe females develop a deep red-orange belly and breast during spawning. The older scientific name Archocentrus multispinosus remains common in the aquarium trade and in many current references.
breedingNotes: The Rainbow Cichlid is among the easiest Central American cichlids to breed in the home aquarium. A bonded pair will clean a flat stone, piece of slate, or section of driftwood before depositing eggs on the surface. Clutch sizes have been reported in the range of 500 to 1,500 eggs; eggs hatch in roughly two days, and fry become free-swimming within about a week. Pairs form monogamous bonds and can spawn repeatedly when conditions remain stable. Biparental brood care — with both parents guarding and tending the young — typically continues for up to around four weeks. Newly hatched brine shrimp and finely crushed flake are generally accepted as first foods.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae / Cichliformes), native range (San Juan ichthyological province of Costa Rica and Nicaragua), max length (17.0 cm TL), wild water envelope (temperature 21–36 C, pH 7.0–8.0, dH 9–20), and diet (detritus, ooze, filamentous algae). Lists Archocentrus multispinosus as a synonym.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked: adult size 12–15 cm (most 7–8 cm), Atlantic and Pacific slope distribution, pair-bonded substrate brooder behaviour, brood size 500–1500 eggs, biparental brood care up to ~4 weeks, diet of flocculent detritus and algae.

fish.breedingNotes: The Rainbow Cichlid is a substrate spawner that deposits eggs on a flat stone or piece of slate after the pair has thoroughly cleaned the site. Spawns commonly number 500 to 1,500 eggs. Eggs hatch in roughly two days, fry become free-swimming within about a week, and both parents tend the brood for up to around four weeks.
